does: This paper introduces the BC Protocol, a structured two-person methodology for generating high-quality chain-of-thought reasoning data for language model training. The approach pairs domain experts with knowledge engineers who systematically externalize implicit expert reasoning through guided dialogue, addressing the expert blind spot problem where practitioners naturally omit steps they consider obvious. The method incorporates a Participant Aptitude Model identifying six dimensions affecting elicitation quality and introduces calibrated ignorance as a technique for extracting tacit knowledge. The protocol is designed to produce complete reasoning chains rather than preference signals, filling a critical gap between crowdsourced annotation (which lacks depth) and solo expert writing (which omits obvious steps).
connects: This work builds on extensive research in knowledge elicitation and machine learning data engineering. It responds to recognized limitations in existing CoT data generation methods: the inadequacy of crowdsourced approaches for complex reasoning, constraints in expert annotation pipelines like those used for instruction tuning, and the reasoning-chain deficit of RLHF-based approaches that only capture preference rankings. The research draws on cognitive science understanding of expert reasoning and the expert blind spot phenomenon, connecting to broader work on tacit knowledge extraction and knowledge engineering methodologies developed in AI knowledge base construction. It also engages with ongoing post-training research that treats high-quality reasoning data as a fundamental bottleneck in LLM capability development.
enables: This protocol establishes a scalable framework for systematic generation of expert reasoning data that could significantly improve language model post-training quality. By operationalizing structured knowledge elicitation with defined participant dimensions, the work enables more consistent and reproducible expert data collection at scale. The methodology could support development of stronger reasoning capabilities in foundation models and specialized expert systems. Future work could adapt the BC Protocol for different domains, integrate it with curriculum learning approaches that leverage detailed reasoning chains, or combine it with synthetic data augmentation to extend limited expert availability. The framework also provides foundations for studying which participant characteristics and elicitation techniques are most effective across different reasoning domains and task types.
